host.note: ARM has no AVX2 intrinsics; runtime selects the scalar path. clang's auto-vectorizer turns the scalar sum/filter loops into NEON code, which is why scalar throughput is high on this host. The CI workflow on ubuntu-22.04 x86_64 exercises the hand-written AVX2 intrinsics.

avx2.note: AVX2 path not available on ARM; this row reflects the scalar fallback the runtime selected. CI will report the real AVX2 numbers on x86_64.
AVX2 path not available on ARM; this row reflects the scalar fallback the runtime selected. CI will report the real AVX2 numbers on x86_64.

scalar.note: Auto-vectorized to NEON by clang. The aggregate kernel is dominated by streaming load + add; both arches saturate DRAM bandwidth on 100M-row inputs.

honest_4_1b_per_sec_claim.reasoning: On a 100M-row int32 column the working set is 400 MB, well outside any cache. The throughput is bounded by DRAM bandwidth: at ~10.7 GB/s per scan (1.344e9 * 4 bytes / 0.0744 s effective, plus bitmap writes) this host's single-core memory bandwidth is the bottleneck. The 4.1B/s target is realistic only when the input is L1- or L2-resident (~16 KiB or ~1 MiB) and when AVX2 saturates the load ports. The bench-smoke CI floor is set at 100M v/s, which both AVX2 and scalar comfortably beat.
